hints.Gallia: 'Gallia' is a proper noun — the subject of the sentence
hints.divisa: 'divisa' is a participle, not the main finite verb

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere/esse' — 'est' here comes from 'esse' (to be)

hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the principal parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the principal parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the principal parts of 'ire' (to go)

hints.Present active participle: Present active participle would be 'dividens' — 'divisa' has the -a ending of a perfect passive participle
hints.Gerundive: Gerundive would be 'dividenda' — 'divisa' is the 4th principal part stem

hints.Ablative: Ablative of 'Gallia' would be 'Gallia' (same form), but it's the subject here
hints.Vocative: Caesar is not addressing Gaul — he's describing it

hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'Gallia' with -a ending is 1st declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have varied endings — 'Gallia' is a typical 1st declension -a noun
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'Gallia' is 1st declension

hints.Genitive: 'omnis' can be genitive singular, but here it agrees with 'Gallia' as nominative
